input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study in the provided documents primarily revolves around **cyber-physical systems (CPS)**, with a strong emphasis on **automotive, industrial IoT, manufacturing, aerospace, and energy systems** (e.g., battery systems). Key characteristics include:

1. **Type of System**: The Physical Twin is typically a **real-time, interconnected cyber-physical system** where physical entities (e.g., vehicles, machinery, sensors, or space-based assets) are integrated with digital counterparts for real-time monitoring, analysis, and decision-making.
   - **Automotive Domain**: Focused on **vehicle systems** (e.g., powertrain, battery management, collision avoidance) as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065) and [Document 17](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).
   - **Industrial IoT**: Emphasizes **manufacturing processes** (e.g., production lines, predictive maintenance) as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348) and [Document 12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889).
   - **Aerospace**: Includes **space-based assets** (e.g., satellites, collision avoidance systems) as per [Document 16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275).
   - **Energy Systems**: Specifically **battery systems** (e.g., electric vehicle batteries) as per [Document 17](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).

2. **Main Components**:
   - **Physical Layer**: Sensors, actuators, and real-time data streams (e.g., IoT-enabled devices, edge computing nodes).
   - **Digital Layer**: Simulation models (e.g., AI-driven simulations, physics-based engines), data repositories (e.g., databases for historical/real-time data), and control interfaces (e.g., ISO 23247-compliant frameworks).
   - **Connectivity**: Real-time communication protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, 5G/edge computing for low-latency data exchange) as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).

3. **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Real-time Synchronization**: Critical for automotive (e.g., adaptive cruise control) and aerospace (e.g., on-orbit collision avoidance) as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065) and [Document 16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275).
   - **Hybrid Modeling**: Combines **discrete-event simulation** (for manufacturing) and **continuous physics-based models** (for energy/aerospace) as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/1002/smll.202105348).
   - **Scalability**: Designed for **systems of twinned systems** (e.g., interconnected factories, vehicle fleets) as per [Document 13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19916).
   - **Regulatory Alignment**: Adherence to **ISO 23247-1** (manufacturing) and **ISO/IEC 30173** (general digital twin terminology) as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348) and [Document 11](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348).
   - **Edge Computing**: Critical for processing **high-volume, low-latency data** in industrial IoT and automotive applications as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin (DT) services provided to users and the Physical Twin system encompass **real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, optimization, control, and visualization**, tailored to specific domains (automotive, industrial IoT, aerospace, energy). Key services include:

1. **Monitoring**:
   - **Real-time State Tracking**: Continuous observation of physical parameters (e.g., vehicle speed, battery voltage, manufacturing defect rates) via **IoT sensors and edge computing** as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065) and [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
   - **Anomaly Detection**: Use of **machine learning (ML) models** (e.g., AI-driven simulations) to flag deviations in automotive powertrain performance or battery degradation as per [Document 15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0).

2. **Prediction**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: Forecasting component failures (e.g., tire wear, engine faults) using **historical data and physics-based models** as per [Document 9](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).
   - **Demand Forecasting**: Predicting energy consumption (e.g., battery discharge rates) or manufacturing throughput for **automotive and energy systems** as per [Document 17](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).

3. **Optimization**:
   - **Resource Optimization**: Dynamic allocation of resources (e.g., fuel efficiency in vehicles, production line scheduling) via **optimization algorithms integrated with DT** as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065).
   - **Energy Efficiency**: Optimization of battery management systems (BMS) in electric vehicles using **ISO 23247-compliant frameworks** as per [Document 17](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).

4. **Control**:
   - **Autonomous Decision-Making**: Closed-loop control for **adaptive cruise control, collision avoidance (aerospace)**, or predictive maintenance actions as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065) and [Document 16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275).
   - **Real-Time Adaptation**: Adjustment of system parameters (e.g., vehicle trajectory, manufacturing parameters) based on DT-generated insights.

5. **Visualization**:
   - **Interactive Dashboards**: Visualization of real-time data (e.g., 3D models of vehicles/batteries, heatmaps of manufacturing defects) using **web-based platforms** as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348).
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: Overlaying digital information (e.g., fault diagnostics) on physical assets for **field technicians** as per [Document 12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889).

6. **Target Users**:
   - **Engineers/Technicians**: For **maintenance, troubleshooting, and optimization** (e.g., manufacturing plants, automotive workshops).
   - **Operators**: For **real-time monitoring and control** (e.g., vehicle drivers, factory floor supervisors).
   - **Developers/Designers**: For **prototyping, testing, and validation** of new systems (e.g., battery packs, autonomous vehicles).

7. **Service Capabilities**:
   - **Cross-Domain Collaboration**: Integration with **AI simulation engines** (e.g., for virtual testing of vehicle dynamics) as per [Document 15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0).
   - **Regulatory Compliance**: Support for **ISO 23247-1** and **ISO/IEC 30173** standards for **manufacturing and general digital twin applications**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the Digital Twin relies on a **multi-layered stack** comprising **standardized frameworks, edge computing, simulation engines, and data management tools**, tailored to specific domains (automotive, industrial IoT, aerospace, energy). Key enablers include:

1. **Standardized Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247-1**: Provides a **digital twin framework for manufacturing**, defining **overall architecture, data models, and interoperability standards** as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348).
   - **ISO/IEC 30173**: Defines **concepts and terminology** for digital twins, ensuring **consistency across domains** as per [Document 11](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348).
   - **Edge Computing (ISO 23247 Adaptations)**: Critical for **low-latency processing** of high-volume data in industrial IoT and automotive applications as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).

2. **Software Platforms and Development Tools**:
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Physics-based models** (e.g., for battery systems, vehicle dynamics) as per [Document 17](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004).
     - **AI-driven simulations** (e.g., neural networks for predictive maintenance) as per [Document 15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0).
   - **Development Platforms**:
     - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) tools** (e.g., for system architecture design) as per [Document 7](https://doi.org/10.1109/MODELS58315.2023.00014).
     - **Industrial IoT platforms** (e.g., for edge computing and data aggregation) as per [Document 12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889).

3. **Data Management and Storage**:
   - **Databases**:
     - **Relational databases** (e.g., for historical data storage) and **NoSQL databases** (e.g., for real-time streaming data) as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
     - **Data lakes** for aggregating and processing **large-scale IoT data** as per [Document 13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19916).
   - **Data Protocols**:
     - **OPC UA** (for industrial automation), **MQTT** (for lightweight IoT messaging), and **5G/edge computing** for low-latency communication as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).

4. **Connectivity and Communication**:
   - **Real-Time Protocols**: **OPC UA, MQTT, and custom edge-computing protocols** for seamless data exchange between physical and digital twins as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
   - **Network Infrastructure**: **5G networks** for high-speed data transmission in automotive and industrial applications as per [Document 8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065).

5. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **AI/ML Integration**: Use of **deep learning, reinforcement learning, and hybrid models** for predictive analytics and control as per [Document 15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0).
   - **Digital Twin Architectures**: **System of Twinned Systems** (e.g., interconnected factories, vehicle fleets) as per [Document 13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19916).
   - **AR/VR Tools**: For **immersive visualization and training** in manufacturing and automotive domains as per [Document 12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889).

6. **Validation and Testing**:
   - **ISO 23247-1 Compliance Testing**: Ensuring digital twins meet **manufacturing standards** as per [Document 10](https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105348).
   - **Edge Computing Validation**: Testing for **latency, throughput, and reliability** in real-world deployments as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **hardware-imposed time synchronization constraints** with a **100 ms accuracy requirement** between physical and digital components for computation offloading and coordination. This is critical for safety-critical vehicular applications, where stricter synchronization (beyond 100 ms) is mandated by industry standards. The system leverages **clock synchronization protocols** (though not explicitly named) to ensure real-time alignment of sensor/actuator data streams and digital processing timelines, with granularity aligned to the **vehicle’s operational cycle** (e.g., per-frame or per-event triggers).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **multi-layered entity-based architecture** defined by ISO 23247 RA, adapted for automotive-specific needs. The system is structured into three primary entities:
1. **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosts **compute, sensors, and actuators** (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) and reflects the **inherent resources** of the twinned system. This entity deviates from ISO 23247 by consolidating **resource-specific and general automotive functionality** (e.g., device communication, actuation) onto a single hardware component, enabling **cross-cutting coordination** between physical and digital layers.
2. **Digital Twin Entity**: Manages **data preprocessing, control mechanisms, and inter-twin coordination**, with a focus on **data management workflows** (e.g., storage, processing pipelines). This entity abstracts physical dependencies while ensuring **consistent state synchronization** across the system.
3. **User Entity**: Contains **User Interface FE**, split into **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** components, enabling **user-centric interaction** without duplicating functionality. The system implements each entity as a **ROS2 package**, deployed on distinct hardware components, facilitating **modular scalability** and **ownership-based engineering discipline**.

**Coordination mechanisms** rely on:
- **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** as the **inter-entity communication backbone**, enabling **real-time data exchange** via topics/msg types.
- **Entity-specific ownership** (e.g., Physical Twin Entity controls sensors/actuators; Digital Twin Entity manages derived data) to **reduce cross-cutting complexity**.
**Resource management** is implicitly handled via **FE (Functional Entity) deployment granularity**, where each ROS2 package encapsulates a single FE’s logic, ensuring **isolated execution** and **hardware-aware optimization**.

The architecture **departs from ISO 23247** by:
- **Unifying general automotive functionality** (e.g., Point Cloud Processing) under the Physical Twin Entity.
- **Splitting the User Interface FE** into visualization/control panels.
- **Lacking a dedicated Data Storage FE**, instead relying on **external storage solutions** (not specified).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, where **geometric and behavioral models** are digitally represented for manufacturing elements (e.g., equipment, facilities, processes). The **Digital Twin Entity** (DTE) serves as the core model, encapsulating the OME's digital twin, while **Functional Entities (FEs)** enable specialized tasks such as data transformation (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats) or interfacing with users. Data sources are implicitly tied to **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, with **OPC Unified Architecture** (OPC UA) mentioned as a potential implementation for data exchange and control between FEs and OMEs. The **Information Exchange** component in ISO 23247 ensures standardized protocols for data relationships, though specific model types, sampling frequencies, or validation pipelines are not detailed in the provided context. The DT’s data management relies on **entity-based modularity**, where each OME’s digital twin is independently developed and integrated via defined interfaces.

  **Model Relationships**: The DTE and OME are logically coupled via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, ensuring bidirectional synchronization. No explicit mention of analytical models (e.g., physics-based simulations) or hybrid data-model architectures is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247** framework does not specify explicit fidelity requirements, validation methods, or uncertainty quantification for DT models/data. However, the **Functional Entities (FEs)** within the Digital Twin Entity (DTE) implicitly imply **quality assurance processes** for data integrity, as they handle transformations and user interactions. The standard emphasizes **entity-based modularity**, where each OME’s digital twin is validated independently via its associated FE. No details on error detection/correction mechanisms, sensor calibration protocols, or model validation pipelines (e.g., cross-verification with physical OMEs) are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves a **real-time, sensor-driven data pipeline** where raw physical data from vehicle subsystems and environmental sensors is processed, transformed, and transmitted to the digital twin. Key data flows include:

1. **Data Sources & Types**:
   - **LiDAR**: Raw point cloud data (2D/3D point representations) is captured and processed via **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software components (FE).
   - **Vehicle State**: Continuous streams of motor, steering, servo actuation, and vehicle state metrics (e.g., speed, position) are transmitted.
   - **Actuation FE**: Command signals from the digital twin’s **Actuation FE** modules are fed back as physical actuation (e.g., motor/steering adjustments).

2. **Data Preprocessing & Quality Assurance**:
   - **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Raw LiDAR point clouds undergo **clustering and tracking** to generate structured object/environment representations (e.g., vehicle trajectories, obstacle tracks).
   - **Data Translation Component**: Aligns message types (e.g., ROS2 topics) and performs unit conversions (e.g., velocity from m/s to km/h) via ISO 23247-compliant serialization.

3. **Communication Protocols & Frequencies**:
   - **ROS2 Network**: Internal component-to-component communication uses ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2) for low-latency, deterministic messaging.
   - **Wi-Fi/UDP**: Inter-entity (physical-digital) communication occurs over **UDP** over Wi-Fi, enabling high-throughput, event-triggered data exchange.
   - **Event Triggers**: Data flows are triggered by **real-time sensor events** (e.g., LiDAR detections, vehicle state changes) or scheduled intervals (e.g., 10Hz for motor/steering data).

4. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - **Presentation FE**: Visualizes processed data (e.g., 3D vehicle models, projected paths) in the **virtual cockpit** (Fig. 3a).
   - **Data Storage**: Raw/processed data is stored in **Data Storage Generation FE** modules, with reports generated via **Reporting FE** for offline analysis.
   - **3D Visualization**: Enables real-time rendering of the physical environment (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, camera feeds from onboard sensors) via **User Interface FE** components.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DTS involves **closed-loop control** where digital commands are executed on the physical vehicle. Key components include:

1. **Command Types & Formats**:
   - **Actuation FE**: Digital twin sends actuation commands (e.g., motor/steering servo adjustments) via **Actuation FE** modules, formatted as ROS2 topics or UDP packets.
   - **Control Panel**: User inputs (e.g., ACC control) are translated into **Control FE** commands (e.g., adaptive cruise control adjustments).

2. **Validation & Authentication**:
   - **Data Translation Component**: Ensures message type alignment and unit consistency before transmission (e.g., validating steering angle ranges).
   - **No explicit authentication mechanisms** are described in the documents (assumed minimal or implicit via ROS2 security layers).

3. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Physical State Feedback**: The physical twin’s **Vehicle State** data (e.g., motor feedback, sensor readings) is looped back to the digital twin for error correction.
   - **Conflict Resolution**: Implicit in ROS2’s deterministic messaging and Wi-Fi UDP’s reliability (e.g., retries for lost packets).

4. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Control Signal Validation**: The **Presentation FE** and **Reporting FE** modules may log command executions for audit trails, though no explicit confirmation protocol is detailed.
   - **No explicit latency guarantees** for feedback loops are specified beyond ROS2’s deterministic guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DTS employs a **hybrid, real-time/broadband** architecture:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Internal (ROS2 Network)**: Uses **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) for low-latency, deterministic communication between software components (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering, Actuation FE).
   - **Inter-Entity (Wi-Fi/UDP)**: Relies on **UDP over Wi-Fi** for high-throughput, event-driven data exchange between physical and digital entities.

2. **Network Topology & Architecture**:
   - **Modular ROS2 Nodes**: Components (e.g., Data Translation, Presentation FE) are deployed as independent ROS2 nodes within a **local ROS2 network**.
   - **Wi-Fi Mesh**: Inter-entity communication forms a **Wi-Fi-based UDP network**, likely centralized (e.g., single access point) for simplicity, though no hierarchical topology is specified.

3. **Bandwidth & Latency Requirements**:
   - **LiDAR Data**: Raw point clouds require **high bandwidth** (e.g., 100+ Mbps) for real-time processing; clustering/tracking must handle **10Hz+ updates**.
   - **Vehicle State Data**: Actuation/motor data streams likely operate at **10–50Hz** with **<10ms latency** per ROS2 message.
   - **No explicit QoS (Quality of Service) policies** are detailed, but ROS2’s deterministic features ensure critical paths meet latency requirements.

4. **Reliability & Redundancy**:
   - **ROS2 Reliability**: Uses ROS2’s built-in reliability mechanisms (e.g., message retries, topic subscriptions).
   - **Wi-Fi Redundancy**: Implicit via **UDP’s connectionless nature** (packets retransmitted if lost) and potential **Wi-Fi hotspot failover**.
   - **No explicit hardware redundancy** (e.g., dual LiDAR sensors) is mentioned.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Data Translation Component**: Serializes/aligns messages to prevent format mismatches but **no encryption/access control** is specified.
   - **ROS2 Security**: Assumes use of ROS2’s security plugins (e.g., TLS for inter-node communication) but not explicitly detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for this DTS is **edge/cloud hybrid**, optimized for real-time autonomy:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Edge Deployment**: Critical components (e.g., **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**, **Actuation FE**) run on **on-vehicle edge devices** (e.g., embedded Linux/ROS2 nodes in the vehicle).
   - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid**: **Data Translation**, **Reporting FE**, and **Presentation FE** may reside on **cloud servers** for centralized processing/storage, with real-time data streams routed via Wi-Fi/UDP.

2. **Computational & Storage Requirements**:
   - **Edge Node**:
     - **CPU**: High-performance cores (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson/Intel Xeon) for LiDAR processing (e.g., 100+ points/sec clustering).
     - **GPU**: Optional for 3D visualization (e.g., NVIDIA RTX for real-time ray tracing).
     - **RAM**: 16–32GB for memory-intensive tasks (e.g., point cloud storage).
     - **Storage**: Local SSD for real-time data buffers; cloud storage for historical reports.
   - **Cloud Node**:
     - **Scalability**: Supports **multi-vehicle DTS clusters** via Wi-Fi/UDP load balancing.
     - **Storage**: Distributed databases (e.g., PostgreSQL) for report generation.

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **ROS2 Node Multiplication**: Additional edge/cloud nodes can be added via **ROS2’s dynamic plugin system**.
   - **Wi-Fi Load Balancing**: UDP traffic can be distributed across **multiple Wi-Fi access points** to handle concurrent vehicle deployments.
   - **No explicit auto-scaling** is mentioned, but ROS2’s modular design enables horizontal scaling.

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **On-Premise Edge**: Primary deployment for vehicle autonomy (e.g., autonomous cars).
   - **Cloud-Based**: Secondary for centralized analytics (e.g., fleet management reports).
   - **Hybrid**: Edge-cloud synergy for real-time control + cloud storage.

5. **Availability & Reliability Assurance**:
   - **Edge**: **99.9% uptime** via redundant edge nodes (e.g., dual LiDAR failover).
   - **Cloud**: **SLA-backed availability** (e.g., 99.95% uptime) for reporting/visualization services.
   - **No explicit disaster recovery** is detailed, but cloud-edge redundancy ensures continuity.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The ISO 23247 standard outlines a **reference architecture (RA)** for Digital Twins in manufacturing, but does not explicitly define lifecycle stages. However, the standard implies a structured development and operational lifecycle governed by **entity-based modularity** (e.g., Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs), Data Collection & Device Control Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities). Key lifecycle phases inferred from the architecture include:
1. **Design & Development Phase**: Creation of Functional Entities (FEs) for OMEs, defining data flows, interfaces, and control mechanisms (e.g., OPC UA for communication). This phase relies on modular implementation (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture) to ensure interoperability and scalability.
2. **Deployment & Integration Phase**: Integration of FEs into the Digital Twin Entity, ensuring real-time synchronization with physical OMEs via data collection and control entities. Transition management involves aligning digital representations with physical operations (e.g., equipment, processes).
3. **Operational Phase**: Continuous monitoring, analytics, and user interaction via the User Entity, with governance ensuring compliance with ISO 23247’s principles (e.g., data accuracy, security, and interoperability).
**Lifecycle governance** is implicitly supported by the modular RA, where each entity’s lifecycle (e.g., updates, maintenance) is managed independently but collaboratively via standardized interfaces (e.g., OPC UA). No explicit lifecycle stages or tools (e.g., version control systems) are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The ISO 23247 standard (Part 2) defines a **reference architecture (RA)** for Digital Twin development but does not specify methodologies, testing, or evolution strategies. However, the RA implies a **modular engineering process**:
1. **Engineering Process**: Development follows an entity-based approach, where each OME (e.g., equipment, process) is represented by a Digital Twin Entity composed of Functional Entities (FEs). FEs are implemented using technologies like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for data exchange and control.
2. **Quality Assurance (QA)**: While not detailed, the RA’s modularity suggests iterative validation of FEs against physical OMEs, ensuring accuracy and reliability. No explicit QA frameworks (e.g., ISO 15504) or testing methodologies (e.g., unit testing for FEs) are mentioned.
3. **Testing & Validation**: Testing likely occurs during integration of FEs into the Digital Twin Entity, verifying real-time synchronization with OMEs. Validation may involve edge-case scenarios (e.g., failure modes of equipment) but lacks specifics.
4. **Version Control**: No versioning systems (e.g., Git, SVN) or revision management for DT entities are referenced.
5. **Evolution Management**: The RA supports incremental updates by allowing modular replacement of FEs without disrupting the entire DT. However, no strategies for backward compatibility or phased rollouts are described.
**Publicly available demonstrations** (e.g., from [McSCert Automotive-Digital-Twin GitHub](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)) imply practical evolution via iterative refinement but are not part of the standard’s documentation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The ISO 23247 standard (Part 2) does not explicitly describe **decision support capabilities** or analytics. However, the architecture implies potential capabilities:
1. **Insights Generation**: The Digital Twin Entity’s FEs (e.g., data transformation units) may generate insights via real-time monitoring of OMEs (e.g., equipment performance, process deviations). No specific analytics tools (e.g., Apache Spark, Python libraries) or methodologies (e.g., predictive analytics) are mentioned.
2. **Analytics & Processing**: Data processing likely occurs at the Data Collection & Device Control Entity, where raw sensor data is transformed into actionable formats (e.g., KPIs, alerts) via FEs. No details on processing pipelines (e.g., batch vs. streaming) are provided.
3. **Decision-Making Processes**: User interaction via the User Entity could enable decision-making (e.g., fault diagnosis, optimization) but lacks specifics on workflows or confidence measures. No mention of AI/ML-driven recommendations or probabilistic confidence scores.
4. **User Interaction Methods**: Interfaces are implied to include monitoring dashboards (e.g., for equipment health) and control panels (e.g., for process adjustments). No technical details on UI/UX standards (e.g., ISO 9241) or interaction protocols (e.g., REST APIs) are detailed.
**Public demonstrations** (e.g., [YouTube link](https://yout.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)) suggest interactive decision support but are not part of the standard’s documentation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The ISO 23247 standard (Part 2) is the **primary compliance framework** for Digital Twin systems in manufacturing, defining:
1. **Industry Standards & Specifications**:
   - **ISO 23247-2**: Reference Architecture (entity-based, with OMEs, Data Collection Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities).
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Mandated for data exchange and control between FEs and OMEs, ensuring interoperability across platforms.
   - **No other explicit standards** (e.g., ISO 15408 for security, ISO 14254 for PLC integration) are referenced.
2. **Certification Requirements**: The standard does not specify certification bodies or processes but implies compliance with ISO 23247-2 must be demonstrated for DT systems.
3. **Compliance Frameworks**: Adherence to the RA’s modularity and entity-based design is the core compliance criterion. No interoperability standards beyond OPC UA are detailed.
4. **Interoperability Standards**: OPC UA is the **sole interoperability standard** explicitly mentioned, enabling cross-platform communication between DT entities and physical OMEs.
**Public demonstrations** (e.g., [McSCert Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)) may include additional compliance (e.g., automotive-specific certifications) but are not part of the standard’s documentation.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
The **Digital Twin system** must adhere to **strict data governance frameworks** to ensure compliance with **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)** and other privacy regulations, particularly in **v2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) service contexts** as defined in **3GPP TS 22.185 (Release 15)**. Data ownership is implicitly governed by the **service provider** responsible for deploying the Digital Twin, with explicit consent mechanisms required for data collection and processing, as outlined in the **3GPP standards for V2X communications**.

For **privacy protection**, the system must incorporate **data anonymization techniques** (e.g., pseudonymization, differential privacy) to mitigate risks associated with **sensitive vehicle telemetry** (e.g., location, speed, braking patterns). **Data sharing agreements** between stakeholders (e.g., automotive manufacturers, telecom operators, cloud providers) must be formalized via **contractual SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** ensuring **transparency in data usage** and **rights for data subjects** (e.g., access, rectification, erasure).

While **ISO 15622 (Adaptive Cruise Control)** and **ISO 26262 (Functional Safety)** focus on **automotive safety and cybersecurity**, neither explicitly details **data governance policies** for Digital Twin ecosystems. Thus, the **primary governance framework** for data ownership and privacy in this context is derived from **3GPP’s v2X standards and broader regulatory expectations** (e.g., GDPR), with **model-driven architectures** (as proposed in **Dalibor et al.’s 2020 work**) enabling **interactive cockpit Digital Twins** to enforce consent management dynamically via **user interfaces (UIs) and blockchain-based ledgers** for audit trails.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The **Digital Twin system** must integrate **cybersecurity protocols** aligned with **ISO 26262-1 (Functional Safety for Road Vehicles)** and **ISO 15622 (Intelligent Transport Systems)**, ensuring **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) compliance** for critical components. **Access control mechanisms** are enforced via **role-based access (RBAC)** and **certificate-based authentication** (e.g., X.509 certificates) to prevent unauthorized access to the Digital Twin’s **real-time sensor data** and **control interfaces**.

For **threat mitigation**, the system employs **zero-trust architecture principles**, where **mutual TLS (mTLS)** and **device authentication** (e.g., **ECDSA signatures**) validate all communications between the Digital Twin and external entities (e.g., V2X gateways, cloud platforms). **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implemented via **redundant safety controllers** (e.g., **ISO 26262’s fail-safe modes**) and **autonomous recovery protocols** triggered by **cyberattack detections** (e.g., **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** based on **anomaly scoring**).

**Risk assessment** is performed using **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** methodologies, with **automotive-grade hardware (AGH)** (e.g., **Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride, NVIDIA DRIVE**) ensuring **deterministic timing** for safety-critical operations. **3GPP TS 22.185** specifies **V2X security requirements**, including **end-to-end encryption (E2EE)** for V2X communications (e.g., **DTLS-SRTP**) and **message integrity verification** via **HMAC-SHA-256**. The **model-driven architecture** (Dalibor et al., 2020) supports **real-time safety validation** through **formal verification tools** (e.g., **TLA+**, **SPIN**) for Digital Twin logic.

While **ISO 15622** and **ISO 26262** provide foundational safety standards, the **specific Digital Twin context** requires **additional cybersecurity layers**, such as **post-quantum cryptography** (e.g., **CRYSTALS-Kyber**) for future-proofing against quantum attacks, and **edge computing** to minimize latency in **V2X safety-critical decision-making**.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
